text: they possessed a definitive purpose that hadn’t been claimed by the ravages of time, dictated directly by the Creators . Though the town’s upkeep wasn’t unusual — many towns with high concentrations of functioning bots had pre-Black Out buildings, in some cases with electricity and running water —the wall set Cheyenne apart. It surrounded the entirety of the Bot District, with a separate section containing the Market. Ten feet tall, it was an amalgamation of mismatched materials, many salvaged from the buildings that once stood outside its borders — sheet metal, wooden boards and planks, bricks, concrete blocks, and corrugated tin roofing . Ronin had only seen such structures erected by humans. This one protected only bots . The main road into Cheyenne followed mostly-buried train tracks. To the north stood the wall, sheltering the pristine buildings within and blocking everything from view save the light cast onto the clouds overhead. To the south were several old factories, now silent and still. A pair of guards waited in front of the roadblock between the two sections of town . One was a synth, the skin peeled off the top of his head to display the polished metal casing beneath. He called himself Reg. The other, Baron, was an earlier model bot, lacking an artificial epidermis. It was humanoid in shape, but its body was disproportioned; it could never be mistaken for an organic. Their rifles were amalgamations of mismatched parts that somehow came together as wholes. The weapons reminded Ronin of the wall in that respect . Ronin’s vision flared and darkened as his optics adjusted to the changing light . “Coming in late, dustwalker,” said Reg. The skull-like gear that Warlord used as his mark was clear on his head- casing . “Warlord doesn’t appreciate visitors after dark,” Baron explained. Its words were tinny, as though echoing within its vocal synthesizers. It wore Warlord’s mark on its chest, the paint faded and flaking . “But you already know that,” Reg concluded. “We’re simply obligated to inform you of the local customs and policies .” “Praise the Creators,” Ronin replied, “for they programmed you with far more patience than I. Warlord actually make you play these games every time, or have you two learned to take delight in it ?” The guards exchanged a glance . Reg lifted his chin. “What’s in your bag ?” “ Scrap .” “I expect vague answers from meatbags, dustwalker. What’s in it ?” Responses flitted through Ronin’s processors, assessed and discarded rapidly. It would be easy to deactivate these two. He had twelve rounds in his rifle; from this range, it wouldn’t take more than three for each bot to knock out their power cells. Even if they managed to get their rifles up quickly enough to return fire, their weapons were just as likely to explode as they were to shoot . And what then? It would accomplish nothing, would fulfill no directives, would bring Ronin no closer to the truth of his core programming . And Warlord had many more bots bearing that symbol in his service . Ronin could turn and walk away. The only thing tying him to Cheyenne were the few belongings left in his dwelling; they could be replaced, but everything in this world was too precious to abandon so readily . “Steel. Copper. Plastic. Damaged power cells .” “Quantities?” demanded Baron . “Will be determined by the scrapper,” Ronin replied . For five-and-a-half seconds, none of them spoke. The wind wailed over the wastes and the distant shouts of humans drifted over from the slums . “We need to send on word and wait for your entry to be approved,” Reg finally said, head twitching to the side once . “Fine. Be sure to send the answer down to Wellington, and explain to Warlord why my haul is being traded there instead.” Ronin turned on his heel. It was only thirty-seven miles away. He could be there by midnight if he maintained an easy pace. But Wellington was a much smaller community, and couldn’t offer the same price for goods as they did here . “Wait, dustwalker .” Ronin halted, slipping a finger behind the trigger guard. Most settlement-dwellers, whether mechanical or organic, were relatively fair in their dealings with him. The reavers kept to the Dust, for the most part. But Warlord’s town wasn’t normal in many ways . “ Enter .” Ronin didn’t immediately remove his finger from the trigger. He’d been pushing for a fight, he realized. Baiting, as humans sometimes called it. He forcibly eased his grip on the rifle and redirected himself toward Cheyenne . “You’d be better served behind the barriers. Picked up sight of you just over two miles out. Easy targets,” he said as he passed between the two guards and through the concrete barricade . They made no reply, but he saw them exchange a glance in his periphery . He walked in the shadow of the wall. The factory to his right had once been a refinery, but Warlord’s bots must have repurposed it; there hadn’t been any oil pumped in this region for at least two hundred years. The chain link fence around it was mostly intact, though it was a patchwork of rusted steel and new sections likely taken from the supply warehouse Ronin had stopped at on his way back to town . Steadily, voices came into audio range. The shouts of humans — most of the ones he could make out were calls for children to return home, as dark was quickly falling — and the drone of conversation from the Market. He didn’t divert any processing power to isolating and amplifying the individual voices. Their words didn’t matter. Everyone was simply doing their best to survive . Ronin just needed to offload his scrap and get repaired. Then he’d pick a new direction and start walking, come morning. He didn’t have to return to Cheyenne if he didn’t want to . As
